successful_strategies.name: Crypto Four-Factor Model (Sparkline Capital / Liu-Tsyvinski-Wu)
successful_strategies.source: Sparkline Capital Research (Kai Wu, 2024) + Liu, Tsyvinski & Wu, Journal of Finance (2022)
data_sources: on-chain active addresses (for intangible value factor)
key_factors: Crypto market factor: cap-weighted portfolio of all liquid crypto, 31.6% CAGR, 0.37 Sharpe
Momentum factor: past 14-day returns, strongest in large-caps with media attention and leverage cascades
Intangible value factor: price-to-network-activity (active addresses, transactions) filters out junk/shitcoins, delivers market-like returns with half the volatility
Size factor: small-cap premium exists but not statistically significant alone; works when quality-screened
successful_strategies.reported_performance: Market factor: 31.6% CAGR, 0.37 Sharpe (7/2017-2/2024). Value factor: Sharpe 2.43, annualized alpha 150.51. Momentum: 13.28% annualized, Sharpe 0.27. Combined size+momentum+value portfolio: 40.67% annualized, Sharpe 1.51. Equal-weighted crypto benchmark: 46.95% annualized, Sharpe 0.51. Value-weighted benchmark: 54.03% annualized, Sharpe 0.73.
successful_strategies.methodology: Fama-French-style factor construction via Fama-MacBeth cross-sectional regressions. Weekly rebalancing with 7-day rolling window. Newey-West standard errors. Studied 31 cryptocurrencies over Dec 2017-Dec 2023. Long-short factor portfolios, long-only model portfolio of 60% BTC + 20% large-cap momentum + 20% small-cap intangible value.

successful_strategies.name: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Crypto Trading with Overfitting Control
successful_strategies.source: Gort, Liu, Sun, Gao, Chen & Wang — arXiv:2209.05559 (2022)
data_sources: 9 technical indicators: RSI, MACD, CCI, Directional Index (DX), Rate of Change (ROC), and others
15 features total including prices, volumes, and technicals
key_factors: Overfitting detection via hypothesis testing: formulate backtest overfitting as a statistical test, estimate probability of overfitting (p-value), reject overfitted agents above threshold
Combinatorial cross-validation simulates many market situations instead of single walk-forward split
DRL agents (PPO/DQN) trained on feature set with buy/sell/hold actions
Agents with lower overfitting probability outperform in out-of-sample testing
successful_strategies.reported_performance: Less-overfitted DRL agents outperformed: (1) more-overfitted agents, (2) equal-weight benchmark, and (3) S&P DBM Index during a testing period (05/2022-06/2022) when the crypto market crashed twice. Demonstrates that overfitting control is the key differentiator, not the model architecture itself.
successful_strategies.methodology: Markov Decision Process formulation. Walk-forward and K-fold cross-validation shown to be insufficient (single regime test, false IID assumption). Combinatorial cross-validation (CCV) tracks degree of overfitting. Agents rejected if probability of overfitting exceeds threshold.

successful_strategies.name: Dynamic Grid Trading (DGT) Strategy
data_sources: Minute-level price data for Bitcoin and Ethereum
key_factors: Traditional grid strategy proven to have zero expected return under simple assumptions
DGT dynamically resets grid positions adapting to market conditions
Adapts to volatility regimes and trend changes automatically
successful_strategies.reported_performance: DGT significantly outperforms both traditional grid trading and buy-and-hold in IRR (internal rate of return) and risk control on minute-level BTC and ETH data from 2021-2024.
successful_strategies.methodology: Dynamic grid positioning with reset mechanism. Comparison against naive grid strategy and BH benchmark. Statistical proof that traditional grid has zero expected value under random walk.

successful_strategies.name: Cointegration-Based Pairs Trading in Crypto
successful_strategies.source: Tadi & Kortchmeski — Studies in Economics and Finance (2021)
data_sources: Minute-binned price data from Bitmex exchange
Best bid/ask quotes for realistic fill simulation
Market trades data for accurate backtesting
key_factors: Dynamic cointegration-based pairs trading with optimal look-back window estimation
Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process half-life calibration for mean-reversion speed
Three cointegration tests: Engle-Granger, KSS, Johansen
Multiple coin pairs improve risk-adjusted profitability
successful_strategies.reported_performance: Exceeds naive buy-and-hold on Bitmex. Strategy achieves reasonably low maximum drawdown. Multiple coin formulations improve risk-adjusted profitability. Class of coins identified with better arbitrage potential.
successful_strategies.methodology: Dynamic look-back window framework. Realistic market microstructure modeling: uses best bid/ask quotes, waits one or more periods after signal generation before executing. Minute-level formation and trading periods.

successful_strategies.name: Support Vector Machine Crypto Price Prediction Strategy
data_sources: Historical OHLCV data from July 2015 to November 2019
Large set of technical indicators derived from price/volume
key_factors: SVM classification outperformed Random Forest, KNN, and logistic regression for 1-hour-ahead direction prediction
Translates class predictions into trading decisions via realistic backtester
Performs well on BTC, ETH, and LTC over 22-month out-of-sample period
successful_strategies.reported_performance: Support vector machines yield the most profitable trading strategies across Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Litecoin. Outperforms the market on average over the 22-month out-of-sample period since January 2018.
successful_strategies.methodology: Classification approach: predict direction of next 1-hour return. Multiple technical indicators as features. Realistic backtester simulating actual trading conditions. Comparison across SVM, RF, KNN, logistic regression.

failed_approaches.approach: Naive OHLCV-based neural network forecasting with walk-forward validation
failed_approaches.reason_for_failure: Single walk-forward split only tests one market regime; no control for false positives. DRL hyperparameter sensitivity means 'lucky' agents pass backtests. Backtest overfitting probability is high. Negative correlation found between prediction F1 score and trading Sharpe ratio (-0.425).
failed_approaches.lesson: Standard ML validation (train/val/test split, K-fold CV) is insufficient for financial time series. Need combinatorial cross-validation or overfitting hypothesis tests. Good forecasting accuracy does NOT imply good trading performance.

failed_approaches.approach: Fixed-horizon forecasting with overlapping time windows and IID assumption
failed_approaches.reason_for_failure: Financial time series violates the IID assumption. Overlapping windows create dependent samples. Fixed forecasting horizon is unrealistic because liquidity and execution timing are uncertain. Models that ace backtests on single-asset time series fail when applied to a universe of assets.
failed_approaches.lesson: Use non-overlapping windows or triple-barrier labeling (De Prado, 2018). Work with asset universes, not single instruments. Replace fixed-horizon forecasting with control theory / reinforcement learning approaches.

failed_approaches.approach: Pure OHLCV feature sets without order book or on-chain data
failed_approaches.reason_for_failure: OHLCV bars contain only averaged noise. They lack information about order flow, bid-ask queues, tick imbalances, and actual market microstructure. Models trained on bars-only data miss the highest-signal features.
failed_approaches.lesson: Incorporate order book data (bids, asks, queue lengths, tick imbalances). On-chain metrics (active addresses, transaction count, realized cap) provide orthogonal signal. Dollar volume is more informative than raw volume.

failed_approaches.approach: Ignoring transaction costs, slippage, and market impact in backtests
failed_approaches.reason_for_failure: Using mid-price fills when real fills cross the spread. Ignoring market impact for position sizing. Not accounting for shorting costs. Small per-trade alpha quickly destroyed by 2-5% slippage and fees in crypto markets.
failed_approaches.lesson: Model costs at 1.5x-2x best estimate. Use actual bid-ask spread data. Apply Almgren-Chriss market impact model. A strategy that looks profitable with 0 costs often loses money with realistic costs.

failed_approaches.approach: Data snooping: testing hundreds of strategy variations and reporting the best one
failed_approaches.reason_for_failure: If 50 independent strategies are tested at 5% significance, ~2.5 false positives are expected by chance. Harvey, Liu & Zhu (2016) argue standard t=2.0 threshold is far too low; suggest t=3.0. Crypto technical trading rules often fail FDR control when tested across 21,000+ rules.
failed_approaches.lesson: Track ALL variations tested, not just the winner. Apply Bonferroni correction or FDR control. Reserve truly untouched out-of-sample data. Use false discovery rate methodology to separate signal from noise.

failed_approaches.approach: Ignoring survivorship bias in crypto backtest datasets
failed_approaches.reason_for_failure: Survivorship bias inflates returns by ~0.9%/year in equities, likely worse in crypto where 80%+ of projects fail. When delisted/rug-pulled coins are excluded, backtests look artificially good.
failed_approaches.lesson: Use survivorship-bias-free datasets. At minimum acknowledge the limitation. Be skeptical of strategies that hold small-cap or obscure tokens with high failure rates.

key_lessons: Factor investing works in crypto: the market, momentum, size, and intangible value factors successfully port from equities to crypto. The Value and Momentum Everywhere (Asness, Moskowitz, Pedersen, 2013) framework extends to digital assets.

Momentum in crypto is strongest at shorter horizons (1-4 weeks, not 1-12 months) and works best in large-cap, liquid assets. Intangible value works best in small-caps, acting as a quality/shitcoin filter.

Backtest overfitting is the single biggest threat to crypto alpha research. Standard ML validation is insufficient. Use combinatorial cross-validation, overfitting hypothesis tests, and track multiple testing corrections.
Data quality matters enormously: order book data > OHLCV, on-chain metrics add orthogonal signal, point-in-time data is essential, survivorship bias must be accounted for.
Cost modeling is critical: crypto has wider spreads, higher slippage, and more volatile execution costs than equities. Model conservatively at 1.5x-2x estimated costs.

Market non-stationarity means strategies must be tested across multiple regime types (bull, bear, high volatility, low volatility, sideways). A backtest during one regime tells you little about another.
Good prediction does not imply good trading: F1 score and Sharpe ratio can be negatively correlated. The translation layer from forecast to execution is where alpha is won or lost.
Portfolio-level strategies (multi-asset, multi-factor) are more robust than single-asset single-strategy approaches. Single-asset concentration is effectively overfitting to one time series.
False discovery rate control is essential: the sheer number of tested strategies in crypto (21,000+ technical rules, countless ML variations) means many apparent alphas are spurious.

The most successful practitioners (Renaissance Technologies, Wintermute, Jump) share: curated proprietary data, scientific culture, heavy infrastructure investment, no human interference with models, and long time horizons for strategy development.
recommended_approach_for_alphaforge: For the V7 AlphaForge subsystem, the research strongly supports a factor-based alpha discovery pipeline with the following design: (1) Use multiple data layers — OHLCV for baseline, order book for microstructure signals, on-chain metrics for fundamental value, and sentiment/macro for regime detection. (2) Adopt the proven crypto factors (momentum at 1-4 week lookback, intangible value via network-activity ratios, market beta timing, and a quality/size screen). (3) Implement combinatorial cross-validation (not single walk-forward) as the primary backtesting methodology, with explicit overfitting probability estimation via hypothesis testing (per Gort et al. 2022). (4) Track all tested feature/parameter combinations and apply FDR control (Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg). (5) Build a realistic cost model incorporating spread, slippage (Almgren-Chriss), and exchange-specific fees. (6) Test explicitly across at least 3 distinct market regimes (bull, bear, sideways) before promoting any factor. (7) Reject single-asset strategies — require universe-level validation across a minimum of 10 liquid assets. (8) Begin with the baseline three-factor model (market + momentum + size), then add intangible value as the fourth factor, before attempting ML-based alpha discovery.
